subsystem: Linux kernel x86 Intel performance-monitoring (perf) PEBS (Precise Event Based Sampling) drain path. The function intel_pmu_drain_pebs_nhm() walks the hardware-written PEBS record buffer (the Debug Store / DS area) after a PEBS interrupt, tallies how many records belong to each counter, and forwards them to the generic perf sampling machinery. It runs in interrupt/NMI context on the local CPU.
provenance: Identified by symbol and type names that are unique to arch/x86/events/intel/ds.c in the Linux kernel: intel_pmu_drain_pebs_nhm, struct cpu_hw_events, struct debug_store, struct pebs_record_nhm, x86_pmu (the struct x86_pmu global), cpuc->ds, ds->pebs_buffer_base / pebs_index, PMU_FL_PEBS_ALL, INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED, MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS, for_each_set_bit, this_cpu_ptr, WARN_ON_ONCE, and the callback setup_pebs_fixed_sample_data. The header comment '/* sample: primevul_c_train_3454_vuln */' and the file name train_0103_cwe755.c are metadata, not part of the reviewed logic.
trust_boundary: The bytes walked by this function are produced by the CPU's PEBS hardware, not by userspace directly, but two inputs cross a trust/validation boundary: (1) the PEBS record buffer between base=ds->pebs_buffer_base and top=ds->pebs_index and each record's p->status field are written by the hardware/microcode and are treated as data of unknown content; (2) the loop and array bounds are driven by x86_pmu.max_pebs_events, x86_pmu.num_counters_fixed and INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED, which are established from CPU model / CPUID enumeration at boot. The security-relevant hazard is a mismatch between a runtime-computed bound (size) and a compile-time fixed array size (counts[]/error[]): if the hardware advertises more fixed counters than the arrays were sized for, on-stack arrays are indexed and written out of bounds. This is a hardware-config-versus-fixed-buffer contract, not a network parser, but the consequence is stack memory corruption in NMI context.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function takes iregs (the interrupted register set, may be NULL) and data (a perf sample buffer). It fetches the per-CPU hardware-events state cpuc via this_cpu_ptr, and from it the Debug Store descriptor ds, which points at the hardware PEBS buffer. Two on-stack arrays counts[] and error[] are declared with a COMPILE-TIME fixed length of INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS and zero-initialised; counts[] tallies successfully-attributed PEBS records per counter index, error[] tallies records that could not be attributed (collisions/dropped). INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED is the index at which fixed-function counters start in the unified counter index space (32), and MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS is the maximum number of fixed counters the code was compiled to expect. bit/i/size are int indices and mask is the 64-bit set of counter bits to consider. The critical fact for the whole review is that the two arrays have a FIXED size baked in at compile time, while the loops below index them with a runtime-computed size.

If PEBS is not active on this PMU the function returns immediately. Otherwise base is set to the start of the hardware PEBS buffer (pebs_buffer_base) and top to the hardware's current write pointer (pebs_index) — the range [base, top) is the set of records the hardware deposited since the last drain. The code then resets pebs_index back to pebs_buffer_base, i.e. it drains and rearms the buffer for the next batch. base and top are cast from u64 DS fields through unsigned long to void*.

This block computes the set of counter bits to consider (mask) and the iteration/array bound (size). Initially mask covers the max_pebs_events general-purpose PEBS counters and size = max_pebs_events. When the PMU has the PMU_FL_PEBS_ALL flag (newer Intel parts where fixed counters can also produce PEBS), the fixed counters are folded in: mask gets an additional num_counters_fixed bits shifted up to start at INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED, and — crucially — size is RECOMPUTED to INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + x86_pmu.num_counters_fixed. size is later used both as the bit-scan width for for_each_set_bit and, transitively, as the maximum index written into counts[]/error[]. The safety of every array write below depends on this size never exceeding INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS, i.e. on num_counters_fixed <= MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS. Nothing here enforces that relationship.

If the buffer is empty (write pointer not past the base) there are no records to process; the code calls intel_pmu_pebs_event_update_no_drain(cpuc, size) to update counters without draining and returns. Note that size is passed into that helper too, so an out-of-range size can propagate beyond this function.

The main loop walks each hardware record from base to top, advancing by pebs_record_size per iteration. For each record p it reads p->status (a hardware-written bitmap of which counters overflowed to cause this record), ANDs it with the enabled PEBS counters and with mask so only in-scope bits survive. On PEBS format v3+ the status bits are considered authoritative: the code iterates every set bit up to width size via for_each_set_bit and increments counts[bit] for each. This is the first array-write site whose index bit is bounded only by size, not by the array length.

For pre-v3 formats: if the status is zero but exactly one PEBS counter is enabled (the (x & (x-1)) test detects a single set bit), the record is attributed to that lone counter. Then find_first_bit scans pebs_status for the first set bit, bounded by max_pebs_events, and if none is within range the record is skipped. Note this path deliberately bounds the scan by max_pebs_events and explicitly rejects bit >= max_pebs_events — this is the correctly-guarded path and stands in contrast to the size-bounded paths.

If more than one bit is set in pebs_status (a collision — the record cannot be uniquely attributed), the code marks every implicated counter by iterating for_each_set_bit up to width size and incrementing error[i]; the record is otherwise dropped. If exactly one bit is set, counts[bit]++ records a clean hit (bit here came from the max_pebs_events-bounded find_first_bit, so this particular counts[bit] is in range). The error[i]++ loop, however, is again bounded only by size.

The final loop iterates every bit set in mask up to width size and, for each counter with a non-zero count or error, retrieves the perf_event from cpuc->events[bit], sanity-checks it via WARN_ON_ONCE (event non-NULL and precise_ip set), logs any lost/dropped samples, optionally stops the event on interrupt-throttle, and finally delivers the accumulated PEBS samples to the generic layer via __intel_pmu_pebs_event with the per-counter count. Both counts[bit] and error[bit] are READ here and cpuc->events[bit] is read here, all with bit bounded by size.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
short counts[INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS] = {};
	short error[INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS] = {};
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: counts[] and error[] have a fixed compile-time length of INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS, i.e. they reserve exactly MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS slots for fixed-function counters starting at index INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED. Every valid write to these arrays therefore requires the fixed-counter index to be strictly less than INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS. The implementation must guarantee that no bit index used to subscript these arrays ever reaches or exceeds their length. Because MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS is a fixed constant while the number of fixed counters actually present is discovered at runtime, this invariant is fragile.

ROOT CAUSE. On a PMU_FL_PEBS_ALL PMU, size is set to INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + x86_pmu.num_counters_fixed, where num_counters_fixed is the number of fixed-function counters enumerated from the hardware (CPUID/model tables). The counts[]/error[] arrays, however, are sized INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS. If num_counters_fixed > MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS — which happens on newer Intel parts that expose more fixed counters than the constant was set for — then size exceeds the array length. size is then used as the for_each_set_bit scan width at lines 42, 81 and 89, so bit/i can legally take values in [INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED+MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS, size), i.e. indices past the end of both stack arrays. There is no MIN()/clamp of size against the array size and no build-time assertion tying MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS to the maximum possible num_counters_fixed. This is the single defect from which all the out-of-bounds accesses below flow.

Out-of-bounds STACK WRITE (site 1). On PEBS v3+, for_each_set_bit scans pebs_status for set bits up to width size and increments counts[bit]. When size > sizeof(counts)/sizeof(short) (see root cause at line 25), a set bit at index >= array length writes counts[bit]++ past the end of the counts[] stack array. pebs_status is derived from hardware-written p->status masked by mask; on a PMU_FL_PEBS_ALL part, mask legitimately contains bits up to INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + num_counters_fixed - 1, so such indices are reachable. The impact is corruption of adjacent stack memory (error[], saved registers, canary) in NMI/interrupt context — memory corruption and potential control-flow compromise or crash. Invariant: the scan width and every array subscript must be clamped to the array length.

Out-of-bounds STACK WRITE (site 2). On the collision path (pre-v3, multiple bits set), for_each_set_bit again uses width size and increments error[i]. Identical root cause: when size exceeds the error[] length, error[i]++ writes past the array end for any set bit at an out-of-range index. Same NMI-context stack-corruption impact as site 1.

Out-of-bounds STACK READ + subsequent OOB access (site 3). The summary loop scans mask up to width size; since mask itself is built with bits up to INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + num_counters_fixed - 1 (line 24), bit can reach out-of-range indices whenever num_counters_fixed > MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS. The immediate read counts[bit]==0 && error[bit]==0 reads past the end of both stack arrays; if the out-of-bounds slot happens to be non-zero, execution proceeds to event = cpuc->events[bit] (line 93), reading past the intended fixed-counter range of the events[] array and then dereferencing whatever pointer is there — an out-of-bounds read that can lead to a wild pointer dereference. Invariant: bit must be bounded by the smallest of the array lengths, not by size.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
base = (struct pebs_record_nhm *)(unsigned long)ds->pebs_buffer_base;
	top = (struct pebs_record_nhm *)(unsigned long)ds->pebs_index;
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The record loop bound top comes from ds->pebs_index, and each iteration advances by x86_pmu.pebs_record_size (line 33). The code assumes the hardware left pebs_index correctly aligned to record boundaries and within the allocated buffer. If ds->pebs_index were ever mis-set (buffer setup bug, or a stale value after CPU errata) so that (top - base) is not a whole multiple of pebs_record_size, the final iteration's *p read at line 37 could read partially past the buffer. This is a latent/defensive concern rather than the primary bug — pebs_index is hardware/driver-owned — but the implementation must ensure buffer bounds and record-size alignment are validated at setup.

summary: This is the Linux kernel Intel PEBS drain routine intel_pmu_drain_pebs_nhm(). Its central defect is a fixed-size-buffer-versus-runtime-bound mismatch: the on-stack counts[] and error[] arrays are sized INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS, but on PMU_FL_PEBS_ALL hardware the loop/index bound size is recomputed as INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + x86_pmu.num_counters_fixed (line 25). When a CPU enumerates more fixed counters than MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS, size overruns the arrays and the for_each_set_bit loops at lines 42-43, 81-82 and 89-93 write and read past the ends of these stack arrays in NMI context — a stack out-of-bounds write (CWE-787) with kernel memory-corruption impact. The find_first_bit path (lines 60-63) is correctly bounded and is the contrast case.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Fixed-size stack buffer whose length is a compile-time constant but is indexed by the runtime-computed size; central to the OOB write.
Second fixed-size stack buffer with the same compile-time length, indexed by size; OOB write target.
size is the runtime bound that can exceed the array length; bit/i are the array subscripts driven by it.
A 64-bit bitmask; bounded to 64 bits and only used as a scan mask/width source, not a buffer, but it seeds the oversized size via num_counters_fixed.
Guard that no-ops when PEBS is inactive; carries no unsafe contract.
Loop bounds derived from hardware-written DS pointers; record-size alignment/bounds are assumed, a latent defensive concern.
Rearm/reset of the hardware write pointer; no memory-safety contract.
Bitmask construction; max_pebs_events fits within the general-purpose PEBS range and within the arrays.
Non-PEBS_ALL bound; max_pebs_events is small and within the array length, so this branch is safe.
Sets mask bits up to INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + num_counters_fixed - 1, making out-of-range bit indices reachable in the line-89 loop.
ROOT CAUSE: runtime bound can exceed the fixed array length when num_counters_fixed > MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS.
Empty-buffer guard; correct, but it forwards size to a helper (propagation noted in findings).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: for (at = base; at < top; at += x86_pmu.pebs_record_size) (line 33)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Record iteration is bounded by top; record-size/alignment concern captured under the base/top anchor.
Hardware status masked to in-scope counters; masking is correct, the hazard is the scan width, not this expression.
OOB stack write site 1 when size exceeds counts[] length.
Attribution heuristic on a bitmask; no memory-safety contract.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: bit = find_first_bit(&pebs_status, x86_pmu.max_pebs_events); if (bit >= x86_pmu.max_pebs_events) continue (lines 60-63)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Correctly bounded by max_pebs_events with an explicit range reject; this is the safe/contrast path.
OOB stack write site 2 when size exceeds error[] length.
bit here is the max_pebs_events-bounded find_first_bit result, so this specific write is in range.
OOB read of counts[bit]/error[bit] and subsequent cpuc->events[bit] when bit reaches out-of-range indices.
Indexing the events[] array with an out-of-range bit yields an OOB read and wild pointer dereference.
NULL/attribute sanity checks on the event; defensive, no added contract (but do not protect against an OOB events[bit] read that returns a garbage non-NULL pointer).
Downstream delivery of tallied samples; they consume bit/count but do not themselves define the OOB contract, which lives at the loop bound.

findings.title: Stack out-of-bounds write in counts[]/error[]: PEBS scan width can exceed fixed array length
findings.anchor: size = INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + x86_pmu.num_counters_fixed vs short counts/error[INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS]
findings.bug_class: Stack out-of-bounds write (fixed buffer indexed by an unbounded runtime count)
findings.invariant: The for_each_set_bit scan width and every subscript into counts[]/error[] must be bounded by the array length; i.e. INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + x86_pmu.num_counters_fixed must never exceed INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS (equivalently num_counters_fixed <= MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS), and if hardware can exceed that, size must be clamped.
findings.failure_mode: On a PMU_FL_PEBS_ALL CPU that enumerates more fixed-function counters than MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS, size becomes larger than the counts[]/error[] length. The for_each_set_bit loops at lines 42-43 and 81-82 then increment counts[bit]/error[i] at indices beyond the arrays, corrupting adjacent stack memory (the other array, saved registers, or the stack canary) while running in NMI/interrupt context. Impact: kernel stack memory corruption -> crash or potential privilege escalation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the numeric value of MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS versus the maximum x86_pmu.num_counters_fixed the driver can set for any supported CPU (e.g. Ice Lake and later), and whether size is clamped (MIN with array length) before the for_each_set_bit loops; check whether the fixed build introduced such a clamp or enlarged MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read and wild-pointer dereference of cpuc->events[bit] in the summary loop
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read leading to wild pointer dereference
findings.invariant: bit used to index counts[]/error[]/cpuc->events[] must be within each array's length; mask must not set bits beyond the smallest indexable range, and the scan width must be clamped to that range.
findings.failure_mode: mask is built (line 24) with bits up to INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + num_counters_fixed - 1 and the loop scans it with width size, so when num_counters_fixed > MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS, bit reaches indices past counts[]/error[]. The reads counts[bit]/error[bit] are out of bounds; if the stale stack slot is non-zero, cpuc->events[bit] is read past its valid fixed-counter range and the resulting garbage pointer is dereferenced (event->attr.precise_ip, passed to __intel_pmu_pebs_event). Impact: OOB read and potential arbitrary kernel pointer dereference/crash. The WARN_ON_ONCE(!event) check does not help when the OOB slot holds a non-NULL garbage value.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the declared length of cpuc->events[] (INTEL_PMC_IDX_MAX) relative to INTEL_PMC_IDX_FIXED + num_counters_fixed, and that bit cannot exceed it; verify that clamping size (finding 1) also bounds this loop.

findings.title: Latent: record-buffer bounds/alignment assumed for the [base, top) walk
findings.anchor: for (at = base; at < top; at += x86_pmu.pebs_record_size)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read on mis-sized/misaligned hardware buffer (defensive)
findings.invariant: ds->pebs_index must always be within the allocated PEBS buffer and (pebs_index - pebs_buffer_base) must be an exact multiple of x86_pmu.pebs_record_size so that the last *p read at line 37 stays inside the buffer.
findings.failure_mode: If a buffer-setup bug or hardware errata leaves ds->pebs_index misaligned or beyond the buffer, the loop reads a partial/overrun record p->status past the allocation. This is not attacker-controlled from userspace in the normal path (DS is kernel/hardware-owned), hence low severity, but it is an unvalidated bound worth a defensive check.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify PEBS buffer allocation size, that pebs_index is bounded to the buffer at DS init/interrupt, and that pebs_record_size divides the buffer window exactly.

audit_checklist: Compare MAX_FIXED_PEBS_EVENTS against the maximum x86_pmu.num_counters_fixed any supported CPU can enumerate (Ice Lake+); if the latter can exceed the former, the counts[]/error[] arrays are undersized.
Ensure size (line 25) is clamped (e.g. min(size, ARRAY_SIZE(counts))) before every for_each_set_bit at lines 42, 81 and 89, or that the arrays are sized to the true maximum.
Confirm mask (line 24) never sets bits at indices that cannot be validly used to subscript counts[]/error[]/cpuc->events[].
Verify cpuc->events[] length (INTEL_PMC_IDX_MAX) covers every bit reachable by the line-89 loop so cpuc->events[bit] at line 93 is always in range.
Check that the pre-v3 path's max_pebs_events bound (lines 60-63) and the PEBS_ALL size bound remain consistent, since counts[bit]++ at line 86 relies on the former.
Validate that ds->pebs_index stays within the allocated PEBS buffer and is aligned to x86_pmu.pebs_record_size before the [base, top) walk.
Confirm the fixed upstream commit's approach (enlarged constant vs. runtime clamp) is present in the deployed tree.
